critic_raw: The RNN vs. logistic regression readmission prediction experiment uses a large dataset (200,000 visits) and a well-specified temporal split. Primary concerns: First, the dataset is 200,000 visits but the unit of analysis is 30-day readmission per index visit. Multiple visits per patient are counted as separate training examples—if a patient with multiple admissions within the 36-month window has admissions in both the training and test splits, their patient-level history is shared across splits. The design specifies stratification by urgency and CCI but does not mention patient-level exclusivity across splits. Second, the RNN processes up to 50 prior visits as a sequence; patients with fewer than 50 prior visits are padded, but the feature for 'days since prior visit' for padded positions is not specified—if padded positions use a default time interval, this encodes spurious temporal structure. Third, the stratification by admission urgency and CCI at the temporal split boundary ensures class balance but may create slight leakage: if the stratification selects which patients appear in the validation and test sets based on their full CCI trajectory (rather than CCI at the split time), future CCI information could influence split assignment.
all_issues_raised: Patient-level exclusivity not confirmed across temporal splits—shared patient history risk
Padded time intervals for visits fewer than 50 may encode spurious temporal structure
Stratification on full CCI trajectory may use future information in split assignment

round_transcripts.critic: The experiment has three concerns: patient-level exclusivity across temporal splits not confirmed; padded time intervals for short-visit-history patients are unspecified; and stratification on CCI may use future CCI information in split assignment.
round_transcripts.defender: On patient exclusivity: the design states all records for a single patient are assigned to one set based on the index admission date, which implies patient-level assignment to splits. This is a good practice that should be made explicit. On padded time intervals: standard LSTM with masking handles padded positions by excluding them from loss computation; the padding value (zero or a sentinel) for time-since-prior-visit does not affect learning if masking is applied correctly. On stratification: CCI at the temporal split boundary should use CCI computed from records before the split point only—this is implicit in temporal splitting but should be explicitly stated. If CCI is computed from the full patient record regardless of split point, future information could leak.
round_transcripts.adversary_note: The masking for padded positions needs explicit specification in the design. The CCI-at-split-boundary computation method needs clarification.

round_transcripts.critic: The patient-level assignment is implicit in the design description and should be made explicit. The masking and CCI-at-boundary concerns are valid specification gaps. With these clarifications, the design would be sound. The main remaining concern is that at 200,000 visits with potential multiple visits per patient, the effective number of independent patients may be substantially lower—and the positive-class rate (~15–20% readmission) on a per-visit basis may double-count high-utilization patients who contribute multiple positive examples, inflating apparent positive-class frequency.
round_transcripts.defender: The double-counting concern for high-utilization patients is valid: patients with frequent admissions and high readmission rates contribute disproportionately to the training signal. This could bias the model toward patterns associated with high-utilization patients rather than typical patients. Reporting performance stratified by number of prior admissions (0–1, 2–5, >5) would diagnose whether this is occurring. The design should be explicit about how the readmission label is assigned per visit vs. per patient.

all_issues_adjudicated: Patient-level exclusivity must be explicitly confirmed in design—not just implied
High-utilization patient overrepresentation in positive class may bias model toward frequent-flier patterns
